schools.summary: The LORD beginning the revelation of the tabernacle: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — God, with Moses in the cloud on the mount (Exodus 24:18), beginning the long revelation of the tabernacle (its offering, pattern, and furniture, chs. 25-31, the divine instruction, the pattern shown to Moses, vv.9, 40, the beginning of the sanctuary-revelation, the providence, God revealing his will for the place of his dwelling among his people); the LORD speaking (the revelation of the tabernacle, the pattern, vv.9) — the divine instruction (the word that began the sanctuary); the LORD revealing the tabernacle (the design, the pattern, the furniture) — the place of God's dwelling, the meeting of God and his people, vv.8, 22, the providence (the revelation of the sanctuary, the type of heavenly things, Hebrews 8:5; 9:23-24) — Henry, Gill, Keil.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD beginning the revelation of the tabernacle
the revelation of the sanctuary, the place of God's dwelling (Exodus 24:18)
the type of heavenly things (Hebrews 8:5; 9:23-24)

schools.summary: The freewill offering for the sanctuary: 'speak unto the children of Israel, that they bring me an offering: of every man that giveth it willingly with his heart ye shall take my offering' — God calling the people to bring an offering (a contribution, the materials for the sanctuary, the offering, the freewill gift for the building of the tabernacle, vv.3-7, the call, the people invited to give for God's house); the willing heart — 'of every man that giveth it willingly with his heart ye shall take my offering': the offering to be freewill, from a willing heart (not compelled, but cheerfully and voluntarily given, the witnesses noting the principle — God loves the willing, cheerful giver, the offering acceptable when given from the heart, 2 Corinthians 9:7, 'God loveth a cheerful giver,' not grudgingly, or of necessity, the lesson — the spirit of acceptable giving, the heart, not the amount, the willing mind, 2 Corinthians 8:12, 'if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted,' the freewill offering for God's house, the people's hearts stirred to give, Exodus 35:5, 21-22, 29, 'as many as were willing hearted'; 1 Chronicles 29:9, 14, 'of thine own have we given thee,' the gladness of giving to God); 'my offering' (the offering taken of the willing) — God's offering (given to him, accepted by him, the providence, the willing offering acceptable, the heart the measure) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill, Ellicott.

debates.question: What is the measure of acceptable giving?

sides.position: The willing, cheerful heart, not the amount - the freewill offering given not by compulsion but from a willing heart (2 Corinthians 9:7, 'God loveth a cheerful giver'; 8:12, 'if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted'); the gladness of giving to God (Exodus 35:5; 1 Chronicles 29:9, 14)

themes: Speak unto the children of Israel, that they bring me an offering: of every man that giveth it willingly with his heart ye shall take my offering
the freewill offering for the sanctuary (the crux of the willing heart)
the freewill offering given from a willing, cheerful heart (2 Corinthians 9:7; 8:12)
the heart, not the amount, the measure of acceptable giving (Exodus 35:5; 1 Chronicles 29:9)

schools.summary: The metals of the offering: 'and this is the offering which ye shall take of them; gold, and silver, and brass' — the offering beginning with the precious metals: gold, silver, and brass (bronze, copper, the materials of the sanctuary, the metals for the furniture, the vessels, the structure, the first and best, the precious metals given for God's house, the providence, the materials, much of it the spoil of Egypt, Exodus 12:35-36, now consecrated to God); the gold and silver (for the ark, the mercy seat, the vessels, the most precious for the holiest things) — the precious materials (the gradation, gold for the holiest, then silver, then brass); 'and brass' (the brass, bronze, for the altar, the laver, the court, the less holy parts, the gradation of the metals, the holiest of gold, the outer of brass, the providence, the materials, the spoil of Egypt consecrated, the first and best for God, the metals later read by the witnesses as figures — gold of the divine glory, silver of redemption, brass of judgment endured) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And this is the offering which ye shall take of them; gold, and silver, and brass
the metals of the offering
the first and best, the precious metals for God's house
the spoil of Egypt consecrated to God (Exodus 12:35-36)

schools.summary: The fabrics of the offering: 'and blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine linen, and goats' hair' — the offering including the rich fabrics: blue, purple, and scarlet (dyed yarns or threads), fine linen (white, costly), and goats' hair (for the coverings, the materials of the curtains, veil, and priestly garments, the rich colors and fine linen for the hangings and vestments, the providence, the costly fabrics consecrated to God); the blue (heavenly), purple (royal), scarlet (the color of blood) — the rich dyes (the witnesses noting the symbolism, blue of heaven, purple of royalty, scarlet of sacrifice, the colors woven into the curtains and the high priest's garments, figures, some say, of the heavenly, kingly, and sacrificial glory of Christ); 'fine linen, and goats' hair' (the fine linen, white, pure, costly, the figure of righteousness, Revelation 19:8, and goats' hair, for the tent-coverings, the materials of the hangings, the providence, the rich and the plain, the costly fabrics for the sanctuary) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine linen, and goats' hair
the fabrics of the offering
the rich colors - blue (heavenly), purple (royal), scarlet (sacrificial)
the fine linen a figure of righteousness (Revelation 19:8)

schools.summary: The skins and wood of the offering: 'and rams' skins dyed red, and badgers' skins, and shittim wood' — the offering including rams' skins dyed red, badgers' skins (or sealskins, durable leather), and shittim wood (acacia, the hard, durable desert wood, the coverings, the skins for the outer coverings of the tent, the protection, and the wood, the acacia, the framework, the boards, the ark, the furniture, the providence, the durable, plain materials for the structure, beneath the rich fabrics); the rams' skins dyed red (the outer covering) and badgers' skins (the topmost, weatherproof covering) — the protection of the tent (the plain, durable outer coverings, over the rich curtains beneath, a figure, some say, of the lowly outward appearance veiling the inner glory, as Christ 'no beauty that we should desire him,' Isaiah 53:2); 'shittim wood' (the acacia, the hard, incorruptible desert wood, for the boards, the ark, the table, the altar, the durable framework, the providence, the incorruptible wood overlaid with gold, a figure, the witnesses note, of the humanity of Christ, incorruptible, joined with the gold of his deity) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And rams' skins dyed red, and badgers' skins, and shittim wood
the skins and wood of the offering
the durable coverings veiling the inner glory (Isaiah 53:2)
the incorruptible acacia overlaid with gold, a figure of Christ's humanity and deity

schools.summary: The oil, spices, and incense of the offering: 'oil for the light, spices for anointing oil, and for sweet incense' — the offering including oil for the lamps (the light of the candlestick, vv.37; 27:20), spices for the holy anointing oil (Exodus 30:23-25), and for the sweet incense (Exodus 30:34-38, the materials of the worship, the oil for the light, the anointing for consecration, the incense for the altar of prayer, the providence, the materials for the daily service of the sanctuary); the oil for the lamps (the light of the holy place, the candlestick, vv.37; 27:20) — the light of the sanctuary (the figure, the oil of the Spirit, the light of the church, Zechariah 4:2-6; Matthew 5:14-16); 'spices for anointing oil, and for sweet incense' (the anointing oil, for consecrating the priests and the holy things, Exodus 30:23-30, the figure of the Spirit's anointing, 1 John 2:20, 27, and the sweet incense, for the altar of incense, the figure of prayer, Psalm 141:2; Revelation 5:8; 8:3-4, the providence, the materials for the worship, figures of the Spirit, the anointing, and prayer) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Oil for the light, spices for anointing oil, and for sweet incense
the oil, spices, and incense of the offering
figures of the Spirit's light and anointing (Zechariah 4:2-6; 1 John 2:20)
the incense of prayer (Psalm 141:2; Revelation 5:8; 8:3-4)

schools.summary: The precious stones for the priestly garments: 'onyx stones, and stones to be set in the ephod, and in the breastplate' — the offering including onyx stones and other precious stones to be set in the ephod (the high priest's garment, with the two onyx stones on the shoulders, Exodus 28:9-12) and in the breastplate (the twelve stones, for the twelve tribes, Exodus 28:17-21, the materials of the high priest's vestments, the providence, the precious stones for the garments of glory and beauty, Exodus 28:2); the onyx stones (the two on the high priest's shoulders, engraved with the names of the tribes, Exodus 28:9-12) — the bearing of the tribes on the shoulders (the high priest bearing the people before God, a figure of Christ bearing his people); 'stones to be set in the ephod, and in the breastplate' (the twelve stones of the breastplate, the twelve tribes, borne on the high priest's heart, Exodus 28:17-29, the figure, the high priest bearing the tribes on his shoulders and heart, a type of Christ bearing his people on his strength and his love, Isaiah 49:16, 'I have graven thee upon the palms of my hands,' the providence, the precious stones for the garments, the tribes borne before God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Onyx stones, and stones to be set in the ephod, and in the breastplate
the precious stones for the priestly garments
the tribes borne on the high priest's shoulders and heart (Exodus 28:9-12, 17-29)
a figure of Christ bearing his people on his strength and love (Isaiah 49:16)

schools.summary: The sanctuary that God may dwell among his people: 'and let them make me a sanctuary; that I may dwell among them' — God commanding Israel to make him a sanctuary (a holy place, the tabernacle, the sanctuary, the holy dwelling, the place of God's presence among his people, the great purpose, vv.8b, that God may dwell among them); the wonder — 'that I may dwell among them': the purpose of the sanctuary, that God himself might dwell in the midst of his people (the witnesses noting the astonishing condescension — the high and holy God, who dwells in the heaven of heavens, 1 Kings 8:27, and who appeared on the mount as devouring fire, Exodus 24:17, stooping to dwell among sinful men, the indwelling God, God in the midst of his people, the great blessing of the covenant, the presence of God with them, Exodus 29:45-46, 'I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will be their God'; Leviticus 26:11-12, the lesson — the dwelling of God with man, the central wonder, advancing through Scripture: the tabernacle, the temple, then the incarnation, the Word made flesh, and 'dwelt, tabernacled, among us,' John 1:14, then the church, the temple of God, the Spirit dwelling in them, 1 Corinthians 3:16; Ephesians 2:21-22, and at last the consummation, 'the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them,' Revelation 21:3, the heart of the gospel, Immanuel, God with us, Matthew 1:23); the sanctuary made that God may dwell among them — the presence of God the glory of the sanctuary (the providence, the dwelling of God with his people, the type of the greater dwelling in Christ and the church and the glory) — Keil, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What is the great purpose of the sanctuary?

sides.position: That God himself might dwell among his people - the astonishing condescension of the holy God dwelling among sinful men (Exodus 29:45-46; 1 Kings 8:27), the central wonder advancing through Scripture: tabernacle, temple, incarnation (John 1:14), church (1 Corinthians 3:16; Ephesians 2:21-22), and the consummation (Revelation 21:3); Immanuel, God with us (Matthew 1:23)

themes: And let them make me a sanctuary; that I may dwell among them
the sanctuary that God may dwell among his people (the crux of the indwelling God)
the astonishing condescension of the holy God dwelling among sinful men (Exodus 29:45-46; 1 Kings 8:27)
the central wonder - tabernacle, incarnation (John 1:14), church (1 Corinthians 3:16), consummation (Revelation 21:3), Immanuel (Matthew 1:23)

schools.summary: The tabernacle made according to the pattern shown: 'according to all that I shew thee, after the pattern of the tabernacle, and the pattern of all the instruments thereof, even so shall ye make it' — the tabernacle and all its instruments (furniture) to be made exactly according to the pattern God showed Moses on the mount (the pattern, the divine model, the heavenly pattern, vv.40, the exactness, nothing left to human invention, all according to God's design, the divine authorship of the sanctuary); the pattern (the model shown to Moses, the heavenly original, Hebrews 8:5, 'the pattern shewed to thee in the mount') — the divine design (the tabernacle a copy of heavenly things, the witnesses note, Hebrews 9:23-24, 'the patterns of things in the heavens'); 'even so shall ye make it' (the exact obedience, the sanctuary made precisely as shown, nothing added or omitted, the lesson, the worship of God to be according to his appointment, not man's invention, the regulative principle, the will-worship condemned, Colossians 2:23, the providence, the pattern divine, the tabernacle a figure of heavenly things, the type of Christ and the church, Hebrews 8:5; 9:23-24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: According to all that I shew thee, after the pattern of the tabernacle, and the pattern of all the instruments thereof, even so shall ye make it
the tabernacle made according to the pattern shown
the divine design, a copy of heavenly things (Hebrews 8:5; 9:23-24)
the worship of God according to his appointment, not man's invention (Colossians 2:23)

schools.summary: The making of the ark of the covenant: 'and they shall make an ark of shittim wood: two cubits and a half shall be the length thereof, and a cubit and a half the breadth thereof, and a cubit and a half the height thereof' — the ark (a chest, of acacia wood, of the given dimensions) the first and chief piece of the furniture (the ark of the covenant, the holiest object, in the most holy place, the throne of God's presence, vv.22, the dwelling-place of the testimony, the tables of the law, vv.16); the ark of acacia wood (overlaid with gold, vv.11) — the chest for the testimony (the chief furniture, the throne of God, the figure of Christ, the witnesses note, the acacia of his humanity, the gold of his deity); the ark of the given size — the chest for the tables of the law (vv.16), with the mercy seat above (vv.17, the holiest object, the providence, the ark the center of the sanctuary, the throne of God, the figure of Christ, in whom the law is kept and over whom is the mercy seat) — Henry, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And they shall make an ark of shittim wood: two cubits and a half shall be the length thereof...
the making of the ark of the covenant
the chief furniture, the throne of God's presence (vv.22)
the figure of Christ - the acacia of his humanity, the gold of his deity

schools.summary: The ark overlaid with gold and crowned: 'and thou shalt overlay it with pure gold, within and without shalt thou overlay it, and shalt make upon it a crown of gold round about' — the ark (of acacia wood) overlaid with pure gold, inside and out, with a golden crown (rim, molding) around the top (the gold, the precious overlay, the glory of the ark, the within-and-without, the gold covering the wood entirely, the crown, the golden rim, the ornament and the holding of the mercy seat); the gold within and without (the wood entirely covered) — the figure (the witnesses note, the acacia of Christ's humanity overlaid within and without with the gold of his deity and glory, pure throughout, no flaw within or without); 'a crown of gold round about' (the golden crown, the rim, the ornament, the figure, the crown of glory, the kingly dignity, the witnesses note, of Christ, the ark crowned, Hebrews 2:9, 'crowned with glory and honour,' the providence, the ark of gold, the throne of God, the figure of Christ in his glory) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And thou shalt overlay it with pure gold, within and without shalt thou overlay it, and shalt make upon it a crown of gold round about
the ark overlaid with gold and crowned
the acacia overlaid within and without with the gold of deity, pure throughout
the ark crowned with glory, a figure of Christ (Hebrews 2:9)

schools.summary: The gold rings for carrying the ark: 'and thou shalt cast four rings of gold for it, and put them in the four corners thereof; and two rings shall be in the one side of it, and two rings in the other side of it' — four gold rings cast and fixed at the four corners (or feet) of the ark, two on each side, for the staves (vv.13-14, the rings, for the bearing of the ark, that it might be carried without touching it, the provision for transport, the ark borne on the shoulders of the Levites, Numbers 7:9; 2 Samuel 6:13); the four gold rings (for the staves, the carrying) — the provision for bearing the ark; 'two rings shall be in the one side of it, and two rings in the other side of it' (the rings on the two sides, for the staves to bear the ark, the providence, the ark to be carried by the staves, not touched, the holiness guarded, Numbers 4:15; 2 Samuel 6:6-7, Uzzah) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt cast four rings of gold for it, and put them in the four corners thereof; and two rings shall be in the one side of it, and two rings in the other side of it
the gold rings for carrying the ark
the provision for bearing the ark by the staves (vv.13-14)
the ark not touched, the holiness guarded (Numbers 4:15; 2 Samuel 6:6-7)

schools.summary: The staves for bearing the ark: 'and thou shalt make staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with gold' — the staves (poles, of acacia wood overlaid with gold) for carrying the ark (the staves, matching the ark, gold-overlaid wood, the provision for bearing, the poles through the rings, vv.14, the holiness, the ark borne, not touched); the staves of acacia (for carrying) — the poles (the bearing of the ark); 'overlay them with gold' (the gold overlay, matching the ark, the provision, the staves through the rings, the ark borne by them, vv.14, the providence, the ark carried by the staves, the holiness guarded, Numbers 4:15) — Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with gold
the staves for bearing the ark
the gold-overlaid poles for carrying the ark
the ark borne not touched, the holiness guarded (Numbers 4:15)

schools.summary: The staves placed to bear the ark: 'and thou shalt put the staves into the rings by the sides of the ark, that the ark may be borne with them' — the staves put through the rings on the sides, that the ark might be carried by them (the bearing, the ark borne on the staves, on the shoulders of the Levites, Numbers 7:9, the holiness guarded, the ark not touched, borne by the poles, Numbers 4:15; 2 Samuel 6:6-7, Uzzah struck for touching it); the staves through the rings (the ark made ready to bear) — the provision (the bearing); 'that the ark may be borne with them' (the ark carried by the staves, on the shoulders, not on a cart, Numbers 7:9; 2 Samuel 6:3, 13, the holiness, the ark not touched, borne by the poles, the providence, the holy ark carried reverently, the staves the appointed means) — Gill.

themes: And thou shalt put the staves into the rings by the sides of the ark, that the ark may be borne with them
the staves placed to bear the ark
the ark carried reverently by the poles, on the shoulders (Numbers 7:9)
the ark not touched, the holiness guarded (Numbers 4:15; 2 Samuel 6:6-7)

schools.summary: The staves never removed from the ark: 'the staves shall be in the rings of the ark: they shall not be taken from it' — the staves to remain always in the rings, never removed (the permanent readiness, the ark always ready to be borne, the staves never taken out, the reverence, the ark not handled, the staves left in, so that none need touch the ark to insert them, Numbers 4:6, the pilgrim character, the ark ever ready to move, the people a pilgrim people, following the ark); the staves always in the rings (never removed) — the permanent readiness (the ark ever ready to be carried); 'they shall not be taken from it' (the staves never removed, the ark not touched, the reverence, the ark ever ready to move with the pilgrim people, the providence, the ark always ready to lead the people, the staves the abiding means, the reverence guarded, cf. 1 Kings 8:8, the staves seen in the temple) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: The staves shall be in the rings of the ark: they shall not be taken from it
the staves never removed from the ark
the ark ever ready to be borne by the pilgrim people
the reverence guarded, the ark not touched (Numbers 4:6; 1 Kings 8:8)

schools.summary: The testimony placed in the ark: 'and thou shalt put into the ark the testimony which I shall give thee' — the ark to receive 'the testimony' (the two tables of stone, the Ten Commandments, vv.21; Exodus 31:18, the law called 'the testimony' as God's witness and the people's witness, the ark of the testimony, the ark named for its contents, the law within, the law in the ark, the moral law kept in the holiest, under the mercy seat); the 'testimony' (the tables of the law, the Ten Commandments, God's testimony, the covenant document) — the law in the ark (the witnesses note the figure — the law in the ark, under the mercy seat, vv.17, 21, a figure of the law kept in the heart of Christ, 'thy law is within my heart,' Psalm 40:8; Hebrews 10:7, applied to Christ, the law magnified and honored, and covered by the mercy seat of his atonement); the testimony (the law) in the ark — the holiest contents (the law kept in the holiest place, the providence, the law in the ark, under the mercy seat, the figure of the law fulfilled in Christ and covered by his propitiation, Romans 3:25; 10:4, 'Christ is the end of the law') — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And thou shalt put into the ark the testimony which I shall give thee
the testimony placed in the ark
the law (the tables of the Ten Commandments) kept in the holiest (Exodus 31:18)
a figure of the law in Christ's heart, covered by his atonement (Psalm 40:8; Hebrews 10:7; Romans 3:25)

schools.summary: The making of the mercy seat of pure gold: 'and thou shalt make a mercy seat of pure gold: two cubits and a half shall be the length thereof, and a cubit and a half the breadth thereof' — the mercy seat (Hebrew kapporeth, the covering, the propitiatory) made of pure gold, the exact size of the top of the ark, to cover it (the mercy seat, the golden lid of the ark, the place of atonement and meeting, vv.22, the covering of the ark, the law within, the mercy seat above); the kapporeth (the covering, the place of propitiation, where the blood was sprinkled on the day of atonement, Leviticus 16:14-15) — the witnesses noting the great type — the mercy seat (Greek hilasterion, the propitiatory, the very word used of Christ, Romans 3:25, 'whom God hath set forth to be a propitiation, hilasterion, through faith in his blood'; Hebrews 9:5, 'the cherubims of glory shadowing the mercyseat,' Christ the mercy seat, the propitiation, the place where God meets the sinner in mercy, the law covered, the blood applied, the wrath appeased, the figure — the mercy seat over the law, the broken law within, the mercy seat of pure gold above, the blood sprinkled on it, God meeting the people in mercy over the covered law, a figure of Christ, in whom mercy and justice meet, Psalm 85:10, 'mercy and truth are met together; righteousness and peace have kissed each other'); the mercy seat (the exact cover of the ark) — the covering of the law (the providence, the mercy seat the place of atonement, the type of Christ the propitiation, Romans 3:25; 1 John 2:2; 4:10) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the mercy seat signify?

sides.position: The place of propitiation (the kapporeth where the blood was sprinkled, Leviticus 16:14-15), a type of Christ the propitiation (Romans 3:25, hilasterion; Hebrews 9:5; 1 John 2:2) - the mercy seat over the covered law, where God meets the sinner in mercy through the blood, mercy and justice meeting (Psalm 85:10)

themes: And thou shalt make a mercy seat of pure gold: two cubits and a half shall be the length thereof, and a cubit and a half the breadth thereof
the making of the mercy seat of pure gold (the seat of propitiation)
the propitiatory where the blood was sprinkled (Leviticus 16:14-15)
a type of Christ the propitiation (Romans 3:25; Hebrews 9:5; 1 John 2:2), mercy and justice meeting (Psalm 85:10)

schools.summary: The cherubim of gold on the mercy seat: 'and thou shalt make two cherubims of gold, of beaten work shalt thou make them, in the two ends of the mercy seat' — two cherubim (angelic figures) of gold, of beaten work (hammered out of one piece with the mercy seat, vv.19), at the two ends of the mercy seat (the cherubim, the angelic guardians of the holiness of God, as at Eden, Genesis 3:24, the ministers of God's presence, Ezekiel 10; Psalm 80:1, 'thou that dwellest between the cherubims,' the figures over the mercy seat); the cherubim (the angelic figures, the attendants of God's throne, the guardians of his holiness) — the figures of the holy angels (the witnesses note the symbolism, the angels who desire to look into the mystery of redemption, 1 Peter 1:12, 'which things the angels desire to look into,' looking down on the mercy seat); 'of beaten work shalt thou make them, in the two ends of the mercy seat' (the cherubim of one piece with the mercy seat, beaten work, hammered out, not separate, vv.19, the unity, the cherubim and the mercy seat one, the providence, the angels over the mercy seat, attending the place of propitiation, the throne of grace, Hebrews 4:16) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And thou shalt make two cherubims of gold, of beaten work shalt thou make them, in the two ends of the mercy seat
the cherubim of gold on the mercy seat
the angelic guardians of God's holiness (Genesis 3:24; Psalm 80:1)
the angels desiring to look into the mystery of redemption (1 Peter 1:12)

schools.summary: The cherubim of one piece with the mercy seat: 'and make one cherub on the one end, and the other cherub on the other end: even of the mercy seat shall ye make the cherubims on the two ends thereof' — one cherub at each end, made of (out of) the mercy seat itself (the same piece of gold, hammered out, not joined on, the unity, the cherubim and the mercy seat one continuous piece, the figure, the angels and the place of propitiation one work, the angels attending the mercy seat); the cherubim at the two ends (facing each other, over the mercy seat, vv.20) — the figures (the attendants of the mercy seat); 'even of the mercy seat shall ye make the cherubims on the two ends thereof' (the cherubim of one piece with the mercy seat, the same gold, not separate, the unity, the angels and the propitiatory one work, the providence, the cherubim attending the mercy seat, the place of God's meeting with man, vv.22) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And make one cherub on the one end, and the other cherub on the other end: even of the mercy seat shall ye make the cherubims on the two ends thereof
the cherubim of one piece with the mercy seat
the angels and the propitiatory one continuous work of gold
the cherubim attending the place of God's meeting with man (vv.22)

schools.summary: The cherubim covering the mercy seat with their wings: 'and the cherubims shall stretch forth their wings on high, covering the mercy seat with their wings, and their faces shall look one to another; toward the mercy seat shall the faces of the cherubims be' — the cherubim spreading their wings upward, overshadowing (covering) the mercy seat, their faces turned toward each other and down toward the mercy seat (the overshadowing, the wings spread over the place of propitiation, the cherubim of glory shadowing the mercy seat, Hebrews 9:5, the gazing, the faces toward the mercy seat, the angels looking on the place of atonement); the wings overshadowing the mercy seat (the cherubim covering, guarding, attending the place of propitiation, Hebrews 9:5) — the overshadowing (the angels attending the throne of grace); 'toward the mercy seat shall the faces of the cherubims be' (the faces toward the mercy seat, the angels gazing on the place of atonement, the blood, the propitiation, the figure, the angels desiring to look into the mystery of redemption, 1 Peter 1:12, the wonder of the angels at the cross, the providence, the cherubim attending and gazing on the mercy seat, the place of God's meeting with man in mercy, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the cherubims shall stretch forth their wings on high, covering the mercy seat with their wings... toward the mercy seat shall the faces of the cherubims be
the cherubim covering the mercy seat with their wings
the cherubim of glory overshadowing the place of propitiation (Hebrews 9:5)
the angels gazing on the atonement, desiring to look into the mystery of redemption (1 Peter 1:12)

schools.summary: The mercy seat set above the ark over the law: 'and thou shalt put the mercy seat above upon the ark; and in the ark thou shalt put the testimony that I shall give thee' — the mercy seat set above on the ark, and the testimony (the law) within the ark (the order, the law within, the mercy seat above, the figure, the law covered by the mercy seat, the broken law beneath the place of propitiation, the blood over the law); the mercy seat above the ark (covering the law within) — the figure (the mercy seat over the law, the propitiation covering the broken law, the wrath appeased over the covered commandments); 'in the ark thou shalt put the testimony that I shall give thee' (the law within the ark, under the mercy seat, the figure, the law in the heart of Christ, Psalm 40:8, covered by the mercy seat of his atonement, Romans 3:25, mercy rejoicing over judgment, James 2:13, the providence, the law within, the mercy seat above, God meeting man in mercy over the covered law) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt put the mercy seat above upon the ark; and in the ark thou shalt put the testimony that I shall give thee
the mercy seat set above the ark over the law
the broken law covered by the mercy seat, the propitiation over the commandments
God meeting man in mercy over the covered law, mercy rejoicing over judgment (Psalm 40:8; Romans 3:25; James 2:13)

schools.summary: God meeting and communing from above the mercy seat: 'and there I will meet with thee, and I will commune with thee from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubims which are upon the ark of the testimony, of all things which I will give thee in commandment unto the children of Israel' — the great promise — 'there I will meet with thee, and I will commune with thee from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubims': God promising to meet and commune with Moses (and through him, his people) from above the mercy seat, between the cherubim (the witnesses noting the meaning — the mercy seat the meeting place of God and man, the place of communion and revelation, the lesson — God meets the sinner not on the bare law, which condemns, but on the mercy seat, the place of propitiation, the blood, over the covered law, the throne of grace, the mercy seat the throne, Hebrews 4:16, 'let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace,' the place of access, God communing with man only at the mercy seat, the propitiation, the figure of Christ, in whom alone God meets the sinner in peace, Romans 3:25; 2 Corinthians 5:19, 'God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself,' the LORD that dwells between the cherubim, Psalm 80:1; 99:1; 1 Samuel 4:4; 2 Kings 19:15, the God of the mercy seat); the voice from between the cherubim (the visible token of God's presence, the shekinah over the mercy seat) — the presence (the place of God's dwelling and speaking, the oracle, the holiest); 'of all things which I will give thee in commandment unto the children of Israel' (the revelation from the mercy seat, God communing his will from the place of mercy, the providence, the mercy seat the place of meeting, revelation, and grace, the figure of Christ, the meeting-place of God and man, the throne of grace, Hebrews 4:16; 9:5; Romans 3:25) — Wesley, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does God's meeting 'from above the mercy seat' signify?

sides.position: The mercy seat the meeting place of God and man, where God meets the sinner not on the bare law but on the propitiation - the throne of grace (Hebrews 4:16), the place of access and communion, the figure of Christ in whom God meets and reconciles the sinner (Romans 3:25; 2 Corinthians 5:19); the LORD that dwells between the cherubim (Psalm 80:1; 99:1)

themes: And there I will meet with thee, and I will commune with thee from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubims which are upon the ark of the testimony
God meeting and communing from above the mercy seat (the crux of the meeting place)
the mercy seat the meeting place, the throne of grace where God meets the sinner on the propitiation (Hebrews 4:16; Romans 3:25)
the figure of Christ in whom God reconciles the sinner (2 Corinthians 5:19); the LORD between the cherubim (Psalm 80:1; 99:1)

schools.summary: The making of the table of shewbread: 'thou shalt also make a table of shittim wood: two cubits shall be the length thereof, and a cubit the breadth thereof, and a cubit and a half the height thereof' — the table (of acacia wood, overlaid with gold, vv.24) for the shewbread (the bread of the Presence, vv.30, the table, in the holy place, on the north side, the furniture for the bread set before God continually, the table of the LORD); the table of acacia (overlaid with gold, vv.24) for the shewbread — the table in the holy place (the figure, the witnesses note, the table of the bread of the Presence, a figure of Christ the bread of life, the communion with God at his table); the table of the given size — for the shewbread (the twelve loaves, the bread of the Presence, vv.30; Leviticus 24:5-9, the providence, the table of the LORD, the bread before God, a figure of Christ the bread of life, John 6:35, and the communion of his table, 1 Corinthians 10:21) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: Thou shalt also make a table of shittim wood: two cubits shall be the length thereof...
the making of the table of shewbread
the table for the bread of the Presence, set before God continually
a figure of Christ the bread of life and the communion of his table (John 6:35; 1 Corinthians 10:21)

schools.summary: The table overlaid with gold and crowned: 'and thou shalt overlay it with pure gold, and make thereto a crown of gold round about' — the table (of acacia) overlaid with pure gold, with a golden crown (rim) around it (the gold, the glory of the table, as the ark, vv.11, the crown, the golden rim, the ornament, and the holding of the bread, the figure, the table of gold, the table of the LORD, the glory of the bread of the Presence); the gold overlay (the table covered with gold, the glory) — the precious table (the figure, the acacia overlaid with gold, as the ark); 'a crown of gold round about' (the golden crown, the rim, the ornament, perhaps to keep the bread from falling, the figure, the crown, the glory and dignity, the providence, the table of gold, the table of the LORD, the bread of the Presence in glory) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt overlay it with pure gold, and make thereto a crown of gold round about
the table overlaid with gold and crowned
the table of the LORD in glory, as the ark (vv.11)
the bread of the Presence in glory, crowned

schools.summary: The border and crown of the table: 'and thou shalt make unto it a border of an hand breadth round about, and thou shalt make a golden crown to the border thereof round about' — a border (a rim, a frame) of a handbreadth made around the table, with a golden crown (molding) on the border (the border, the frame, perhaps to strengthen the table or hold the bread, the golden crown, the ornament on the border, the workmanship, the table adorned and made firm); the border (a handbreadth, the rim or frame around the table) — the strengthening (the ornament); 'a golden crown to the border thereof round about' (the golden crown on the border, the second molding, the ornament, the workmanship, the table adorned with gold, the providence, the table of the LORD made beautiful and firm, the bread of the Presence honored) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make unto it a border of an hand breadth round about, and thou shalt make a golden crown to the border thereof round about
the border and crown of the table
the frame strengthening and adorning the table
the table of the LORD made beautiful and firm

schools.summary: The gold rings for carrying the table: 'and thou shalt make for it four rings of gold, and put the rings in the four corners that are on the four feet thereof' — four gold rings made and put at the four corners, on the four feet of the table, for the staves (vv.27-28, the rings, for the bearing of the table, that it might be carried, the provision for transport, the table, like the ark, borne by staves through rings); the four gold rings (for the staves, the carrying) — the provision for bearing the table; 'the four corners that are on the four feet thereof' (the rings at the corners on the feet, for the staves, the providence, the table carried by staves, the furniture borne in the wilderness journey, the pilgrim sanctuary ever ready to move) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make for it four rings of gold, and put the rings in the four corners that are on the four feet thereof
the gold rings for carrying the table
the provision for bearing the table by staves (vv.27-28)
the pilgrim sanctuary ever ready to move

schools.summary: The rings placed for the staves of the table: 'over against the border shall the rings be for places of the staves to bear the table' — the rings placed near (over against) the border, as the places (holders) for the staves to bear the table (the placement, the rings positioned for the staves, the provision for carrying, the staves through the rings, the table borne, the workmanship, the rings set for the bearing); the rings near the border (positioned for the staves) — the placement (the provision); 'for places of the staves to bear the table' (the rings for the staves, the holders, the table borne by the staves, the providence, the table carried in the journey, the furniture borne by the Levites, Numbers 4:7-8) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Over against the border shall the rings be for places of the staves to bear the table
the rings placed for the staves of the table
the rings positioned to hold the staves
the table borne by the staves, carried in the journey (Numbers 4:7-8)

schools.summary: The staves for bearing the table: 'and thou shalt make the staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with gold, that the table may be borne with them' — the staves (of acacia overlaid with gold, as the ark's) for carrying the table (the staves, matching the table, gold-overlaid wood, the provision for bearing, the table carried by the staves through the rings, the pilgrim sanctuary, the table ever ready to be moved); the staves of acacia overlaid with gold (for carrying) — the poles (the bearing of the table); 'that the table may be borne with them' (the table carried by the staves, the providence, the furniture borne in the journey, the pilgrim sanctuary, the table of the LORD ever ready to move with the people) — Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make the staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with gold, that the table may be borne with them
the staves for bearing the table
the gold-overlaid poles for carrying the table
the pilgrim sanctuary ever ready to move

schools.summary: The golden vessels of the table: 'and thou shalt make the dishes thereof, and spoons thereof, and covers thereof, and bowls thereof, to cover withal: of pure gold shalt thou make them' — the vessels of the table (dishes for the bread, spoons or pans for the incense/frankincense, covers and bowls for the drink offerings or to cover the bread) all of pure gold (the furniture of the table, the vessels for the service of the shewbread, Leviticus 24:5-9, the gold, the precious vessels of the LORD's table); the dishes, spoons, covers, bowls (the vessels for the bread, the incense, the drink offerings) — the furniture of the table (the service of the shewbread); 'of pure gold shalt thou make them' (the vessels of pure gold, the precious furniture of the table, the providence, the vessels of the LORD's table, all gold, the glory of the sanctuary, the service of the bread of the Presence) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make the dishes thereof, and spoons thereof, and covers thereof, and bowls thereof, to cover withal: of pure gold shalt thou make them
the golden vessels of the table
the precious vessels for the service of the shewbread (Leviticus 24:5-9)
the furniture of the LORD's table, all of pure gold

schools.summary: The shewbread set before God continually: 'and thou shalt set upon the table shewbread before me alway' — the shewbread (Hebrew 'bread of the face,' the bread of the Presence, the twelve loaves, Leviticus 24:5-9) set on the table before the LORD continually (the bread of the Presence, the twelve loaves, for the twelve tribes, set before God always, renewed every sabbath, the continual offering, the bread ever before God, the figure of the people presented before him); the shewbread (the bread of the Presence, the twelve loaves, the tribes before God, Leviticus 24:5-9) — the figure (the witnesses note, the twelve loaves the twelve tribes presented before God, and the bread a figure of Christ, the bread of life, John 6:35, 48, the true bread before God, the food of his people, the communion of the saints, 1 Corinthians 10:17, 'we being many are one bread'); 'before me alway' (the bread continually before God, ever present, renewed weekly, the figure, the people ever before God, Christ ever presented, the perpetual communion, the providence, the bread of the Presence the figure of Christ and his people before God always, the communion of the table, 1 Corinthians 10:16-17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

themes: And thou shalt set upon the table shewbread before me alway
the shewbread set before God continually
the twelve tribes presented before God always (Leviticus 24:5-9)
a figure of Christ the bread of life and the communion of his people (John 6:35; 1 Corinthians 10:17)

schools.summary: The making of the golden candlestick of one piece: 'and thou shalt make a candlestick of pure gold: of beaten work shall the candlestick be made: his shaft, and his branches, his bowls, his knops, and his flowers, shall be of the same' — the candlestick (the lampstand, the menorah) of pure gold, of beaten work (hammered out of one solid piece), with its shaft, branches, bowls, knops (knobs), and flowers all of the same piece (the candlestick, the seven-branched lampstand, in the holy place, vv.37, the light of the sanctuary, the one piece, the whole of beaten gold, undivided); the candlestick of pure gold, beaten work (one piece) — the precious lampstand (the figure, the witnesses note, the candlestick a figure of the church, the light-bearer, Revelation 1:20, 'the seven candlesticks are the seven churches,' or of Christ the light, John 8:12, the light shining in the holy place, the light of the Spirit, Zechariah 4:2-6); 'shall be of the same' (the whole of one piece, the shaft, branches, ornaments all hammered from one mass of gold, the unity, the candlestick one, though many-branched, a figure of the unity of the church or of Christ and his members, one body, 1 Corinthians 12:12, the providence, the golden lampstand, the light of the sanctuary, the figure of the church or of Christ the light) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And thou shalt make a candlestick of pure gold: of beaten work shall the candlestick be made: his shaft, and his branches, his bowls, his knops, and his flowers, shall be of the same
the making of the golden candlestick of one piece
the light of the sanctuary, all of one beaten piece of gold
a figure of the church the light-bearer (Revelation 1:20) or of Christ the light (John 8:12)

schools.summary: The six branches of the candlestick: 'and six branches shall come out of the sides of it; three branches of the candlestick out of the one side, and three branches of the candlestick out of the other side' — six branches coming out of the central shaft, three on each side, making (with the central shaft) seven lamps in all (vv.37, the seven-branched lampstand, the menorah, the central shaft and six branches, the light, the seven lamps, the perfect light of the sanctuary); the six branches (three on each side, from the central shaft) — the lampstand (the seven lamps, with the shaft); 'three branches... out of the one side, and three branches... out of the other side' (the symmetry, three and three, with the central shaft, seven, the figure, the seven, the perfect number, the fullness of light, the seven lamps the seven Spirits of God, Revelation 4:5, or the fullness of the church's light, Revelation 1:20, the providence, the seven-branched lampstand, the full light of the sanctuary) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And six branches shall come out of the sides of it; three branches of the candlestick out of the one side, and three branches of the candlestick out of the other side
the six branches of the candlestick
the seven-branched lampstand, the full light of the sanctuary (vv.37)
the seven lamps, the seven Spirits of God (Revelation 4:5)

schools.summary: The almond ornaments of the branches: 'three bowls made like unto almonds, with a knop and a flower in one branch; and three bowls made like almonds in the other branch, with a knop and a flower: so in the six branches that come out of the candlestick' — each branch having three bowls (cups) shaped like almonds (or almond-blossoms), with a knop (knob) and a flower (the ornaments, the almond-shaped cups, knobs, and flowers adorning each branch, the almond, the figure, some note, of the almond that buds first, the early-waking, watchful tree, Jeremiah 1:11-12, 'I see a rod of an almond tree... for I will hasten my word'; Aaron's rod that budded with almonds, Numbers 17:8, the beauty of the lampstand); the almond-shaped bowls (the cups, the ornaments) — the beauty (the almond figure); 'a knop and a flower... so in the six branches' (the knops and flowers, the ornaments, repeated on each branch, the symmetry, the same on all six branches, the providence, the beautiful lampstand, the almond ornaments, the watchful light, the church or Christ the light, adorned with the fruit of the watchful word) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Three bowls made like unto almonds, with a knop and a flower in one branch... so in the six branches that come out of the candlestick
the almond ornaments of the branches
the almond-shaped cups, knobs, and flowers adorning each branch
the almond the watchful tree (Jeremiah 1:11-12; Numbers 17:8)

schools.summary: The almond ornaments of the central shaft: 'and in the candlestick shall be four bowls made like unto almonds, with their knops and their flowers' — the central shaft (the candlestick proper) having four almond-shaped bowls, with their knops and flowers (more than the branches, which had three each, the ornaments of the shaft, the central stem adorned, the chief part, the symmetry, the shaft and branches all adorned with almond-cups, knops, and flowers, the beauty of the lampstand); the four almond bowls on the shaft (the central stem, more adorned than the branches) — the ornaments (the chief stem); 'with their knops and their flowers' (the knops and flowers, the adornment of the shaft, the symmetry, the whole lampstand adorned, the providence, the beautiful golden lampstand, the central shaft and the branches, the light of the sanctuary, the figure of Christ or the church the light) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And in the candlestick shall be four bowls made like unto almonds, with their knops and their flowers
the almond ornaments of the central shaft
the central stem adorned with four almond-cups, knops, and flowers
the beautiful golden lampstand, the light of the sanctuary

schools.summary: The knops joining the branches to the shaft: 'and there shall be a knop under two branches of the same, and a knop under two branches of the same, and a knop under two branches of the same, according to the six branches that proceed out of the candlestick' — three knops (knobs) on the central shaft, each under a pair of branches, where the six branches came out (the knops, the points of junction, where the branches joined the shaft, the structure, the six branches springing from the shaft at three knops, the whole united, the symmetry of the lampstand); the knops under the branches (the points where the branches joined the shaft) — the junctions (the structure); 'according to the six branches that proceed out of the candlestick' (the six branches from the shaft, at the three knops, the unity, the branches and shaft one, joined at the knops, the providence, the lampstand one, the branches united to the shaft, a figure of the members united to Christ or to one another, John 15:5, the one light from the one stem) — Gill.

themes: And there shall be a knop under two branches of the same... according to the six branches that proceed out of the candlestick
the knops joining the branches to the shaft
the branches springing from the shaft at three knops, the whole united
a figure of the members united to the one stem (John 15:5)

schools.summary: The whole candlestick of one beaten work: 'their knops and their branches shall be of the same: all it shall be one beaten work of pure gold' — the knops and branches of the same piece, the whole candlestick one beaten work of pure gold (hammered from a single mass, not joined of parts, the unity, the entire lampstand one solid piece of beaten gold, the workmanship, the whole of one mass, undivided, hammered into form, the figure, the unity of the church or of Christ and his members, one body, one light); the knops and branches of the same (one piece with the shaft) — the unity (the one mass); 'all it shall be one beaten work of pure gold' (the whole one beaten work, one solid piece of pure gold, hammered, not cast or joined, the unity, the lampstand one, the figure of the unity of the church, one body in Christ, Ephesians 4:4-6; 1 Corinthians 12:12, the providence, the golden lampstand, the light of the sanctuary, one piece, the figure of the one church the light-bearer, or of Christ the light) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Their knops and their branches shall be of the same: all it shall be one beaten work of pure gold
the whole candlestick of one beaten work
the entire lampstand one solid piece of beaten gold, undivided
a figure of the one church the light-bearer, one body in Christ (Ephesians 4:4-6; 1 Corinthians 12:12)

schools.summary: The seven lamps giving light in the sanctuary: 'and thou shalt make the seven lamps thereof: and they shall light the lamps thereof, that they may give light over against it' — the seven lamps (on the seven branches, the central shaft and six branches) made and lit, to give light in the holy place (the seven lamps, the light of the sanctuary, burning continually, Exodus 27:20-21; Leviticus 24:2-4, the light, the holy place lit by the lampstand, the only light, no window); the seven lamps (the perfect light, the seven, the fullness) — the figure (the witnesses note, the seven lamps the seven Spirits of God, Revelation 4:5, the fullness of the Spirit's light, or the light of the church, Revelation 1:20, the light of testimony in the world, Philippians 2:15); 'that they may give light over against it' (the lamps giving light, over against the lampstand, lighting the holy place, the table, the altar of incense, the figure, the light of the sanctuary, the figure of the Spirit's light, the church the light of the world, Matthew 5:14-16, the providence, the seven lamps the light of the holy place, the figure of Christ or the Spirit or the church the light) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And thou shalt make the seven lamps thereof: and they shall light the lamps thereof, that they may give light over against it
the seven lamps giving light in the sanctuary
the full light of the holy place, burning continually (Exodus 27:20-21)
a figure of the Spirit's light (Revelation 4:5) and the church the light of the world (Matthew 5:14-16)

schools.summary: The golden instruments of the lampstand: 'and the tongs thereof, and the snuffdishes thereof, shall be of pure gold' — the tongs (snuffers, for trimming the wicks) and the snuffdishes (for the snuffings, the trimmed wick-ends) of pure gold (the instruments of the lampstand, for the daily trimming and tending of the lamps, the gold, the precious instruments, all gold, the glory of the sanctuary); the tongs (snuffers, for trimming the wicks, keeping the light bright) — the instruments (the tending of the lamps, a figure, some note, of the tending and trimming needed to keep the light of testimony bright); 'the snuffdishes thereof, shall be of pure gold' (the snuffdishes, for the snuffings, of pure gold, the providence, the instruments of the lampstand all gold, the glory of the sanctuary, the careful tending of the light) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the tongs thereof, and the snuffdishes thereof, shall be of pure gold
the golden instruments of the lampstand
the snuffers and snuffdishes for trimming and tending the lamps
the careful tending to keep the light bright

schools.summary: The talent of pure gold for the candlestick: 'of a talent of pure gold shall he make it, with all these vessels' — the candlestick, with all its vessels (lamps, tongs, snuffdishes), made of a talent of pure gold (a large weight, about a hundred pounds, the talent, the great mass of gold for the lampstand and its instruments, the value, the precious, costly lampstand, all of one talent of pure gold); the talent (a large weight of gold, for the whole lampstand) — the costly material (the precious lampstand); 'shall he make it, with all these vessels' (the lampstand and all its vessels, of the one talent of gold, the providence, the precious golden lampstand, the light of the sanctuary, all of pure gold, the glory and cost of the sanctuary) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Of a talent of pure gold shall he make it, with all these vessels
the talent of pure gold for the candlestick
the lampstand and all its vessels made of a talent (about a hundred pounds) of pure gold
the precious golden lampstand, the costly light of the sanctuary

schools.summary: The charge to make all according to the pattern in the mount: 'and look that thou make them after their pattern, which was shewed thee in the mount' — Moses charged to make all the furniture (and the tabernacle) exactly after the pattern shown him in the mount (the charge, the exact conformity to the divine model, the pattern, the heavenly original, shown to Moses, vv.9, the warning, 'look,' take heed, that nothing be added or altered); the heavenly pattern — the model shown to Moses in the mount (the witnesses noting the New Testament use — the Epistle to the Hebrews citing this very word to prove the tabernacle a copy of heavenly things: 'who serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things, as Moses was admonished of God when he was about to make the tabernacle: for, See, saith he, that thou make all things according to the pattern shewed to thee in the mount,' Hebrews 8:5, the tabernacle a figure, a copy, a shadow of the true, the heavenly sanctuary, Hebrews 9:23-24, 'the patterns of things in the heavens... the figures of the true; but into heaven itself,' the lesson — the earthly sanctuary modeled on the heavenly, a shadow of Christ and the heavenly realities, the worship of God according to his appointment, not man's invention, the regulative principle, all according to the pattern, nothing of man's devising); 'which was shewed thee in the mount' (the pattern shown in the mount, the heavenly model, vv.9, the providence, the tabernacle a copy of heavenly things, the shadow of Christ and the true sanctuary, Hebrews 8:5; 9:23-24, the worship according to God's appointment) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the pattern shown in the mount signify?

sides.position: The tabernacle a copy of heavenly things, modeled exactly on the pattern shown to Moses (cited Hebrews 8:5) - a figure, shadow, and copy of the true heavenly sanctuary (Hebrews 9:23-24), a shadow of Christ and the heavenly realities; the worship of God according to his appointment, not man's invention

themes: And look that thou make them after their pattern, which was shewed thee in the mount
the charge to make all according to the pattern in the mount (the crux of the heavenly pattern)
the tabernacle a copy and shadow of heavenly things (Hebrews 8:5; 9:23-24)
a shadow of Christ and the true sanctuary, worship according to God's appointment
